main_summary.summary: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, interviewed by John Dittmer in Baltimore on March 18, 2013, traces her path from a leftist West Indian household in Providence, Rhode Island into SNCC field organizing in Georgia and Alabama. Her father, an optometrist born in St. Vincent, served as Rhode Island state chairman of the Progressive Party and faced deportation threats after Henry A. Wallace's 1948 campaign; HUAC pressure drove a family friend to suicide. After enrolling at the University of Michigan in 1961, she joined SDS, attended the April 1962 SDS-SNCC meeting in Chapel Hill, and worked with Bernard Lafayette Jr. and the Detroit Friends of SNCC during Wayne State summer classes with C.L.R. James's Facing Reality circle (George Rawick, Seymour Faber). In summer 1963 she defied her parents and joined Charles Sherrod's Southwest Georgia Project in Albany before going to Greenwood, Mississippi to work with Sam Block and Willie Peacock on voter registration. She attended the 1963 March on Washington, the 1964 MFDP challenge in Atlantic City, and joined the Selma project in early 1965, working on poverty-fund challenges, union drives at the Pepsi plant, and voter canvassing through the Selma-to-Montgomery period. She co-edited Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC (Illinois, 2010).
key_themes: SNCC voter registration in Georgia and Mississippi
Progressive Party family heritage and Cold War repression
Preventive nonviolence and armed community defense
main_summary.historical_significance: Noonan is one of a small number of Northern Black women who joined SNCC field projects as undergraduates between 1962 and 1965, and her account provides first-person testimony on three distinct SNCC theaters: Sherrod's Albany/Southwest Georgia project, the Greenwood Mississippi voter-registration drive under Sam Block and Willie Peacock, and the Selma ward-by-ward organizing alongside the SCLC voting-rights campaign. Her testimony documents the 1948 Progressive Party deportation threat against her West Indian-born father, the Detroit Friends of SNCC circle around Bernard Lafayette Jr. and C.L.R. James's Facing Reality group, the Fay Bellamy account of the Forman-Carmichael pre-Bloody-Sunday meeting at the Selma dentist's house, and the Lowndes County Freedom Organization founding discussion where she argued for a black star rather than the panther symbol. As co-editor of Hands on the Freedom Plow, she also speaks to the documentary recovery of SNCC women's accounts.

chapters.title: Providence Family and Progressive Party
chapters.summary: Born in Providence to a St. Vincent-born optometrist father and an Ann Arbor-born mother, Noonan describes her parents' Progressive Party activism. Her father served as Rhode Island state chairman; after Henry A. Wallace's 1948 campaign, immigration authorities threatened him with deportation despite his naturalized citizenship, and a jewelry-factory-owner family friend committed suicide after being called before HUAC.

chapters.title: Integrating Providence Schools
chapters.summary: Noonan recounts being the first Black student at Lexington Avenue Elementary School and the only Black student at the Lincoln School for Girls, a Quaker prep school. She describes being stoned moving into a white neighborhood, the Glee Club exclusion, attending Winter Street AME Zion Church, and her mother's instruction to answer racial slurs with "I'm black and I'm proud."

chapters.title: University of Michigan and SDS
chapters.summary: Her mother relocated to Detroit so Noonan could qualify for in-state tuition at Michigan, where she joined the Methodist Student Movement and then SDS as the only Black member of the campus chapter. Tom Hayden, returning from the Albany freedom rides, brought her and four others to the April 1962 SDS-SNCC meeting in Chapel Hill, North Carolina, which she identifies as a founding moment for both the anti-war and student civil rights movements.

chapters.title: Wayne State and Detroit Friends of SNCC
chapters.summary: Her parents barred her from going South in 1962, so Noonan enrolled at Wayne State, where she took courses with George Rawick and Seymour Faber of C.L.R. James's Facing Reality group. Afternoons she worked with Bernard Lafayette Jr. and Elizabeth Hirschfeld on the Detroit Friends of SNCC, alongside John Watson, Luke Tripp, and Mike Hamlin. The summer culminated with a Diane Nash speaking event nine months into her pregnancy.

chapters.title: Albany Project Under Sherrod
chapters.summary: In summer 1963 Noonan left Michigan against her parents' wishes and joined Charles Sherrod's Southwest Georgia Project. Orientation in Sumter County included news of a young woman's rape and murder by 13 white men. When white drivers circled the church where SNCC women had taken sanctuary, JoAnn Christian's father and cousin Monroe sat on the porch with rifles. Her mother contacted Neil Staebler, Burke Marshall, and Robert Kennedy to demand her protection.

chapters.title: Preventive Nonviolence
chapters.summary: Noonan defines "preventive nonviolence" as practiced in Sherrod's Albany project: Sherrod would notify local gangs of upcoming demonstrations, and they would position themselves between white crowds and nonviolent marchers. SNCC workers rode in cars and stayed in homes with armed community members. She credits Mr. Christian and his cousin sitting armed on the church porch with preventing attacks during the Albany sanctuary episode.

chapters.title: Greenwood Voter Registration
chapters.summary: Defying Sherrod's protective instruction to stay in Albany, Noonan and Jean Wheeler took a bus to Greenwood, Mississippi, where Sam Block and Willie Peacock picked them up. She canvassed for voter registration, helped organize a late-summer voting day with 200-300 attempts, and witnessed white men driving past the SNCC office with guns drawn. She notes that essentially no one was successfully registered despite repeated attempts.

chapters.title: Women in SNCC and March on Washington
chapters.summary: Noonan rejects the framing of women being mistreated in SNCC, describing herself as encouraged to do as much as the men. She describes the August 1963 March on Washington as feeling "too civilized" after Mississippi, recalls Roy Wilkins's disparaging remark about W.E.B. Du Bois who died that day, and notes the censorship of John Lewis's speech for being too critical of the Kennedy civil rights bill.

chapters.title: MFDP Challenge at Atlantic City
chapters.summary: Noonan opposed the 1964 Freedom Summer project on grounds that it abandoned the principle of Black student organizers in Black communities. She attended the Atlantic City convention with the MFDP, recalls Fannie Lou Hamer's testimony, the Council of Churches representative's visible shame, and the offer of two appointed seats to Edwin King and Aaron Henry. She frames the MFDP challenge as evidence that two years of activism had put the vote on the national agenda.

chapters.title: Selma Ward Organizing
chapters.summary: Beginning early 1965, Noonan worked weekends in a Selma ward while studying Black college students' vocational aspirations with Pat Gurin, Doris A. Derby, and John H. Bracey Jr. at Tuskegee. Her ward work included pursuing a stoplight, getting Black women hired at Dan River Mills, and supporting unionization at the Pepsi plant with an AFL-CIO organizer. The going wage for day work in Selma was $12 for a six-day week.

chapters.title: SCLC, Bloody Sunday, and the U-Turn March
chapters.summary: Noonan describes the SCLC entry into Selma against the backdrop of SNCC's prior community-organizing presence and her suspicion that the MFDP challenge prompted SCLC's voting-rights pivot to forestall a populist SNCC-led party. She recounts the "U-turn March" after Bloody Sunday and the Fay Bellamy account in Hands on the Freedom Plow of James Forman and Stokely Carmichael's middle-of-the-night meeting at Dr. Sullivan Jackson's house pleading with King to defy the federal injunction.

chapters.title: Lowndes County and Black Power
chapters.summary: After the Selma-to-Montgomery march, federal registrars eliminated registration barriers and SNCC organizing shifted to Lowndes County, Alabama. Noonan attended the founding discussion of the Lowndes County Freedom Organization, where she preferred a black star symbol (drawing on Marcus Garvey and Nkrumah's Ghanaian Airlines) over the panther. She read the black power slogan as continuous with SNCC's original mission of building vehicles of Black political power.

chapters.title: Mayor Smitherman and Wilson Baker
chapters.summary: Noonan describes the SNCC office sitting directly across from the Selma police station, with prisoners visible through the office window. She kept Wilson Baker's home phone number and called him for protection from a hostile Halloween crowd; he sent cars to escort the SNCC workers home. Mayor Smitherman engaged the SNCC organizers cordially over the poverty-funds challenge, in contrast to the state troopers who attacked marchers on the Edmund Pettus Bridge.

chapters.title: Marriage, New Communities, and Post-SNCC
chapters.summary: Noonan met her first husband Silas Norman Jr. on the Selma project; they married in 1967. They visited Sherrod's New Communities Inc cooperative farm in Albany, where the farmhouse bore bullet holes, and decided the risk to their infant son was too great. Silas attended medical school; she entered a Michigan history PhD program. She raised three sons in inner-city Detroit and stayed active in post-SNCC retrospectives and an effort to free Curtis Hayes Muhammad from jail in Liberia.

chapters.title: Hands on the Freedom Plow
chapters.summary: Noonan describes her co-editorial role on Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC, published 2010 by the University of Illinois Press. The collection gathers 52 essays by women active in SNCC and connected projects, including white and Black women, two Hispanic women, and middle-aged activists alongside the predominantly college-student majority.
related_events: Hands on the Freedom Plow publication 2010

loc_video.caption: Martha Prescod Norman Noonan oral history interview conducted by John Dittmer in Cockeysville, Maryland, 2013 March 18
